left-right-join.label: LEFT and RIGHT JOIN
left-right-join.summary: Include unmatched rows with LEFT and RIGHT JOIN.

sketch0.title: What are LEFT and RIGHT JOINs?

sketch-left-right-join-intro.title: What are LEFT and RIGHT JOINs?
sketch-left-right-join-intro.bodyMarkdown:
When combining tables in SQL, sometimes you want to keep all rows from one table—even if there’s no matching row in the other table. This is where **LEFT JOIN** and **RIGHT JOIN** come in.

- **LEFT JOIN** returns all rows from the left table, plus matching rows from the right table. If there’s no match, columns from the right table will be NULL.
- **RIGHT JOIN** is the opposite: it returns all rows from the right table, plus matching rows from the left table. If there’s no match, columns from the left table will be NULL.

Example: To list all students and any enrollments they have (even if some students are not enrolled in any course):

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```

This query includes every student, even those with no enrollments (their `course_id` will be NULL).

sketch-left-right-join-visual.title: Visualizing LEFT and RIGHT JOINs
sketch-left-right-join-visual.bodyMarkdown:
Imagine two circles representing two tables:

- **LEFT JOIN**: The result includes all rows from the left circle, plus any overlapping part from the right.
- **RIGHT JOIN**: The result includes all rows from the right circle, plus any overlapping part from the left.

In our dataset:
- `students` LEFT JOIN `enrollments` keeps all students, even those not enrolled.
- `enrollments` RIGHT JOIN `students` (if supported) would keep all students, too, but the join direction matters in more complex queries.

**Note:** SQLite does not support RIGHT JOIN directly, but the concept is important and can be simulated by swapping table order in a LEFT JOIN.

quiz-left-join-basic.title: Understanding LEFT JOIN
quiz-left-join-basic.prompt: What does a LEFT JOIN do when joining two tables?
quiz-left-join-basic.hint: Think about which table's rows are always included.

help.concept: A LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table, even if there is no match in the right table. If there is no match, columns from the right table are NULL.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN always includes every row from the left table.
help.hint_2: If a row in the left table has no match in the right, it still appears in the result with NULLs for the right table's columns.

options.a: Returns only rows with matches in both tables.
options.b: Returns all rows from the left table, and matching rows from the right table.
options.c: Returns all rows from the right table, and matching rows from the left table.
options.d: Returns only unmatched rows from both tables.

quiz-left-join-sql-output.title: LEFT JOIN Output Example
quiz-left-join-sql-output.prompt:
Suppose you run this query:

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```

What will be shown for students who are not enrolled in any course?
quiz-left-join-sql-output.hint: What happens to columns from the right table if there's no match?

help.concept: When a LEFT JOIN finds no match in the right table, the columns from the right table are filled with NULL for those rows.
help.hint_1: Unenrolled students still appear, but course_id will be empty (NULL).
help.hint_2: LEFT JOIN includes all students, but if no enrollment is found, course_id is NULL.

options.a: Their name and a NULL value for course_id.
options.b: Their name and a random course_id.
options.c: Their name will not appear at all.
options.d: Their name and every course_id in the enrollments table.

quiz-right-join-concept.title: RIGHT JOIN Concept
quiz-right-join-concept.prompt: Which statement best describes a RIGHT JOIN?
quiz-right-join-concept.hint: Focus on which table's rows are always included.

help.concept: A RIGHT JOIN keeps all rows from the right table, even if there is no match in the left table. If there is no match, columns from the left table are NULL.
help.hint_1: RIGHT JOIN always includes every row from the right table.
help.hint_2: If a row in the right table has no match in the left, it still appears in the result with NULLs for the left table's columns.

options.a: It returns all rows from the right table, and matching rows from the left table.
options.b: It returns only rows with matches in both tables.
options.c: It returns all rows from the left table, and matching rows from the right table.
options.d: It returns only unmatched rows from the right table.

quiz-left-join-code.title: Write a LEFT JOIN Query
quiz-left-join-code.prompt: Write a SQL query to list all courses and the name of the department they belong to. Include courses even if their department is missing (assume some data might be incomplete). Show columns: course title and department name.
quiz-left-join-code.hint: Use LEFT JOIN between courses and departments.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN allows you to keep all courses, even if some have no matching department. The department name will be NULL if missing.
help.hint_1: Start with courses, then LEFT JOIN departments using department_id.
help.hint_2: Select courses.title and departments.name, joining on courses.department_id = departments.id.

quiz-left-join-drag-order.title: LEFT JOIN Query Order
quiz-left-join-drag-order.prompt: Arrange the steps to perform a LEFT JOIN between students and enrollments to show all students and their enrollments (if any).
quiz-left-join-drag-order.hint: Start with SELECT, then FROM, then the LEFT JOIN.

help.concept: A SQL query typically starts with SELECT, followed by FROM, then JOIN clauses, and ends with a semicolon.
help.hint_1: SELECT comes first, then FROM, then the LEFT JOIN.
help.hint_2: The JOIN clause comes after FROM, and the semicolon ends the statement.

tokens.t3: LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id

quiz-left-right-join-fill-blank.title: Fill in the JOIN Type
quiz-left-right-join-fill-blank.prompt:
Fill in the blank to keep all rows from the left table:

```sql
SELECT * FROM students ____ enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```
quiz-left-right-join-fill-blank.hint: Which JOIN type keeps all rows from the left table?

help.concept: the missing term keeps all rows from the left table, even if there is no match in the right table.
help.hint_1: You want to keep all students, even if they have no enrollments.
help.hint_2: the missing term is the correct type for this scenario.

quiz-left-right-join-fill-blank.template: SELECT * FROM students ____ enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
